title: Chocolate hot cross buns
subtitle: Enriched Easter buns with dark chocolate chips
excerpt: Enriched yeasted buns for Easter with dark chocolate chips in place of dried fruit. Soft, glossy, and deeply spiced, with the traditional flour cross on top. Takes the afternoon to make properly.
sourceNotes: Hot cross buns are documented in English domestic cookery from the eighteenth century, traditionally eaten on Good Friday. This version substitutes dark chocolate chips for the traditional dried fruit. The enriched dough formula (butter, eggs, milk, sugar) and the cross method follow the standard British domestic recipe.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed bag once cooled. Warm in a low oven to defrost.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are baked. They dry out quickly — freeze any surplus.

glossaryTerms.definition: A yeast dough that contains butter, eggs, milk, or sugar in addition to flour, water, and yeast. The fat and sugar tenderise the crumb and slow fermentation, which is why enriched doughs take longer to prove than a lean dough made with flour and water alone.

content.text: (with butter, eggs, and milk) is the reason these buns are soft and pillowy rather than chewy. Enrichment also slows the yeast, so the bulk ferment takes longer than a lean dough. Plan for a full afternoon.

Combine the {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{instant-yeast}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{ground-cinnamon}}, and {{cocoa-powder}} in a large bowl. Add the warm {{whole-milk}} and {{free-range-eggs}}. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 5 minutes. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} a little at a time, kneading it in fully before adding more. Continue kneading for a further 5–8 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic. Knead in the {{dark-chocolate-chips}}.
Shape into a ball, place in an oiled bowl, and cover. Leave at room temperature for 1.5 hours until almost doubled.

Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces (about 90 g each). Shape each piece into a tight ball by folding the sides under and rolling on the work surface. Place close together on a greased baking tray in a 3×4 grid. Cover and leave for 1 hour until puffed and the buns are touching.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Mix the {{plain-flour}} with enough water to make a thick, pipeable paste (about 5–6 tablespoons). Transfer to a piping bag or a small ziplock bag with a corner snipped. Pipe a cross over each bun.
Bake for 18–20 minutes until risen and the base sounds hollow when tapped. While still warm, brush with the {{golden-syrup}} for a sticky glaze. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before pulling apart.
